children.name: Testudo
children.description: The Mediterranean tortoises — the classic domed, high-shelled land tortoises of southern Europe, North Africa, and the Middle East. Testudo species have been kept as pets for centuries and were traded extensively across the ancient world; Roman soldiers carried them as a food source on campaign. All are under significant conservation pressure from habitat loss, collection for the pet trade, and the slow reproductive rate typical of long-lived reptiles.

children.name: Agrionemys
children.description: A monotypic genus containing Horsfield's Tortoise — the only tortoise of the Central Asian steppes, named after the naturalist Thomas Horsfield. Unlike its Mediterranean relatives it inhabits semi-arid grasslands and spends much of the year in torpor: active for only two to three months in spring, it spends the rest of the year in deep burrows escaping both summer heat and winter cold. It is the most widely kept tortoise in the world.

children.name: Kinixys
children.description: Hinged-back tortoises of sub-Saharan Africa — unique among tortoises in possessing a rear hinge on the carapace that allows the back of the shell to be lowered to protect the hindlimbs. Several species reach the savanna and open woodland at the southern edge of the Palearctic zone and are among the most ecologically diverse of African tortoises.

children.name: Geochelone
children.description: Large continental tortoises with a broad distribution across South Asia and sub-Saharan Africa. The Indian Star Tortoise is named for the striking radiating yellow pattern on each scute — a pattern that breaks up the tortoise's outline in dappled grassland light and is among the most beautiful in the family.
